macro_segments.title: The Rabbinic Discretion in Determining the Parameters of Biblical Law: The Case of the Firstborn Animal's Raising Period

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Tanya Nami Hachi: Deriving the Thirty and Fifty Day Periods from Pidyon Haben
The Concept of Shemasru HaKatuv LaChachamim: When Biblical Law Parameters are Left to Rabbinic Discretion
Broader Examples of Rabbinic Discretion: Chol HaMoed, Yom Kippur Afflictions, and the Lamed Tes Melachos

macro_segments.title: The Dispute Between Rabbi Yossi and the Sages Regarding the Raising Period for Small and Large Animals

micro_segments.title: The Tannaitic Statement: Thirty Days for Small Animals, Fifty Days for Large Animals
Rabbi Yossi's Position: Three Months for Small Animals Due to Labor Intensity
The Reasoning: Small Teeth and the Need for Extended Care in Grazing Animals

macro_segments.title: The Prohibition Against Early Transfer of the Firstborn to a Kohen: The Issue of Apparent Payment for Services

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Rule: Cannot Give Firstborn to Kohen Within the Raising Period Unless He Will Use It Immediately
Rav Sheshet's Explanation: It Appears Like a Kohen Helping in the Threshing Floor (Exchange of Services)
The Logic: If Kohen Raises Animal for Next Twenty Days, It Appears to Be Payment for Services

macro_segments.title: Terumah and Ma'aser Cannot Be Given as Payment for Services: The Tannaic Statement and Its Scope

micro_segments.title: The Baraita: Those Helping (Kohanim, Leviyim, Poor People) Cannot Receive Terumot and Ma'asrot as Wages
The Principle: Would Desecrate the Institution of Terumah and Ma'aser as Free Gifts
The Distinction: Real vs. Apparent Exchange of Services (Nireh Kemaseya)
Rabbinic Concerns: The Need to Prevent Even Appearances of Improper Exchanges to Avoid Abuse

macro_segments.title: The Torah's Values Regarding Work, Dignity, and Charity: A Talmudic Perspective on Payment for Services

micro_segments.title: Student Question: Why Prevent Work-for-Welfare When It Maintains Dignity and Self-Sufficiency?
Response: The Institution of Terumot and Ma'asrot vs. General Employment Principles
Clarification: Acknowledging Kohanim and Leviyim as Religious Functionaries, Not Farmhands

macro_segments.title: The Case of Choosing a Recipient for Terumah: When Money Can Be Given for Discretion

micro_segments.title: The Permitted Case: Non-Kohen Israelite Paying for the Right to Direct Terumah to a Chosen Kohen
The Prohibited Case: Kohen Paying for Terumah to Go to Another Kohen Appears Like Purchasing the Terumah
The Distinction: Payment for Discretion vs. Payment for the Object Itself

macro_segments.title: Kedushah Status and the Rationale for Different Rules: Why Terumah Permits Discretion Payments But Others Do Not

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Question: Why Only Mention Terumah When Discussing the Discretion Payment Loophole?
The Answer: Terumah Has Kedushat HaGuf (Intrinsic Sanctity) That Prevents Confusion With Monetary Exchange
Other Gifts (Bechor, Zero'a Lechayayim Vekeiva) Have Less Kedushah and Risk Appearing as Purchases
Conclusion: Only Allow Discretion Payment by Terumah to Prevent Degradation of Status for Other Gifts

macro_segments.title: Terumah Outside the Land of Israel: Lenient Rabbinic Status and Practical Implications

micro_segments.title: Rava's Ruling: Terumah from Outside Israel Is Only Rabbinic, So Service Payment Restrictions Do Not Apply
Examples: Rav Chama and Rav Huna Bar Rav Yehoshua Using Terumah Chutz La'Aretz for Service Payment
Shmuel's Leniencies: Terumah Chutz La'Aretz Is Nullified in a Majority (Betela BeRov)

macro_segments.title: Further Leniencies for Terumah Outside Israel: Proactive Bittul and Eating While Tamei

micro_segments.title: Rabba the Kohen: Proactively Nullifying Terumah in a Majority to Eat It While Tamei
Rav Huna Bar Rav Yehoshua: Two-to-One Ratio Method for Mixing Chulin and Terumah Wine
Shmuel's Extreme Leniency: Can Eat Entire Grain Pile and Separate Terumah Afterward (Ochel VeHoleich)

macro_segments.title: Restrictions on Eating Terumah Chutz La'Aretz While Tamei: Only Tumah Yotzei Migufo Is Prohibited

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's Principle: Terumah Chutz La'Aretz Forbidden Only for Those With Tumah Yotzei Migufo (Internal Bodily Flows)
Definition: For Men (Zav, Ba'al Keri) and Women (Niddah, Zavah) — Personal Physical States

macro_segments.title: Rabbinic Discretion in Terrumah Chutz La'Aretz: Balancing Institution of Israel's Kedushah

micro_segments.title: Why Have Terumah Outside Israel At All? Supporting Kohanim and Leviyim Living Nearby
The Tightrope: Protecting Israel's Institution by Making Chutz La'Aretz Lenient Enough to Not Undermine It

macro_segments.title: Restrictions on Eating Terumah Chutz La'Aretz While Tamei: Only Tumah Yotzei Migufo Is Prohibited (continued)

micro_segments.title: Eating vs. Touching: Restriction Applies Only to Eating, Not Touching Terumah

macro_segments.title: Practical Application: A Woman Who Is Niddah Separating Challah for Her Kohen Son

micro_segments.title: The Case: Niddah Woman Cannot Eat Challah But Can Touch It and Give to Minor Kohen Son
Tosafot's Question: Why Specifically a Minor Son? Broader Interpretation Possible
Alternative: If No Minor Available, Burn the Challah and Separate Another One
The Two Challot Principle: Ensuring the Institution of Challah Separation Is Not Forgotten

macro_segments.title: The Woman on the Boat: Questioning Whether Tamei Meis Can Eat Terumah After Immersion

micro_segments.title: The Question: Can Someone Tamei Meis Immerse and Eat Terumah Chutz La'Aretz Without Waiting for Nightfall?
Rav Nachman's Response: Do We Have Parah Adumah Ashes? No, So Why Wait for Nightfall?
Rav Amram's Ruling (Not Accepted): Tamei Meis Can Immerse and Eat Terumah Immediately
Tosafot's Interpretation: Don't Even Need to Immerse—Just Don't Eat If Tumah Yotzei Migufo
The Underlying Logic: Land of Chutz La'Aretz Is Inherently Tamei, So Restrictions Must Be Minimal

macro_segments.title: The Firstborn Must Be Eaten Within Its Own Year: Distinguishing Calendar Year from Biographical Year

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Bechor Should Be Eaten Shana Beshana (Year by Year)
Rav's Interpretation: Its Year Means the Animal's Own Year (Birthday to Birthday), Not Calendar Year
The Verse-Based Derivation: 'Shana Beshana' as a Year That Enters Into Another Year (Cross-Calendar)

macro_segments.title: Alternative Interpretation From the School of Rav: Eating Span and Equivalence to Shelamim

micro_segments.title: Debei Rav's Derasha: Shana Beshana Teaches That Bechor Can Be Eaten Over Two Calendar Years
New Halacha: Bechor Is Eaten for Two Days and an Intervening Night (Like Shelamim)
The Source: Torah Links Bechor to Breast and Thigh of Shelamim, Which Are Eaten for Two Days
Objection: Could Be Linked to Todah (One-Day Offering) Instead
Response: The Phrase 'Lach Yihyeh' Gives Extra Ownership to Bechor, Implying Two Days

topical_tags.term: Lamed Tes Melachos (39 Melachos on Shabbat)
